text: Four 8mm robotic trocars are inserted into the patient's abdomen, with the robotic system docked from the patient's left side.
The surgeon begins dissection at the sacral promontory, visibly dissecting the right side of the peritoneum using robotic instruments.
It is important to preserve the autonomic nose during the dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection in the rectovaginal plane, progressing along the anterior side of the mesorectum down to the pelvic floor.
The surgeon performs a vaginal digital examination, using gloved fingers to palpate and assess the anatomical structures within the vaginal canal.
The surgeon mobilizes the rectum ventrally, carefully dissecting it away from the posterior wall of the vagina using robotic instruments.
A polypropylene mesh measuring ten by three centimeters is inserted into the surgical field.
The surgeon places the first two stitches to anchor the posterior vaginal wall to the lower end of the polypropylene mesh, securing the mesh in position.
The surgeon fixes the ventrally mobilized rectum to the sacral promontory using a polypropylene mesh secured with three O non-absorbable sutures.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum using three OV-lock sutures, completing the surgical procedure.
The polypropylene mesh is positioned extra-peritoneally to minimize potential complications, with its placement visible outside the peritoneal cavity.
